message.content:
Implement EXACTLY ONE task end-to-end in your isolated git worktree, run the gate, commit on the task branch, return the structured result JSON. Do NOT mutate the ledger; do NOT merge.

Task id: T101 (ledgerRefs goals:G6)
Branch: implement/T101    Base commit: 8d8fa77 (current main HEAD)
headline: Add wanbond_session_established metric, last-handshake age, and a 'session established' log line (I2)
description: Add a WG-session signal to the metrics plane: a wanbond_session_established gauge (0/1) and wanbond_session_last_handshake_seconds (age), sourced at scrape time from the amneziawg engine (IpcGet last_handshake_time_sec, or peer lookup as deviceRehandshake in internal/device/failover.go:181 already does). Extend the metrics.Source seam (internal/metrics/metrics.go) with a session snapshot supplied by the device layer; the bind stays WG-unaware. Emit ONE INFO 'session established' log record on the 0→1 transition (poll at probe cadence or scrape-driven with a device-side edge detector). This is the signal that distinguishes 'still converging' from 'wedged' — D35/D36/D37 all presented identically without it.
acceptance: Unit tests cover metric registration and the 0→1 edge; netns e2e asserts wanbond_session_established transitions 0→1 after tunnel up (scraped via metrics.Fetch) and that the path-up-before-session-established ordering holds by comparing the path-up and session-established transition TIMESTAMPS recorded in the logs — NOT by observing a path_up=1/session=0 intermediate scrape (the netns tier reaches it within ms; a scrape observer would nondeterministically miss it; the ~25s gap is a production/WAN artifact); the 'session established' record appears exactly once per session; go test ./... and -tags e2e suite green.

IMPORTANT: the -tags e2e netns suite needs privileged hardware you CANNOT run here — write the e2e assertions so they COMPILE and vet (`go vet -tags e2e ./test/e2e` + `go build -tags e2e ./test/e2e` must pass) and note e2e execution is deferred (G2 pattern). Validate the metric registration + 0→1 edge via runnable unit tests.

Gate (run via `nix develop -c sh -c '<cmd>'`): `go build ./... && go vet ./... && go vet -tags e2e ./test/e2e && go test ./...`. gofmt your changes. Read internal/metrics/metrics.go (the Source seam + PathSnapshots pattern), internal/device/metrics.go (how device supplies the snapshot), and internal/device/failover.go:181 (deviceRehandshake peer-lookup / last_handshake_time_sec).

If your worktree is not reachable for edits, `git switch -c t101-fix implement/T101` in your own worktree via the shared .git store, commit, report the EXACT sha. Mutation-verify the 0→1 edge test discriminates. Return {taskId, status, resultCommit, branch, filesTouched, checkSummary, summary, blockedReason?} with the exact resultCommit sha.

- baboon: Baboon data model compiler reference. Covers model syntax, multi-version evolution, code generation, wire-format conversions, database migrations, backward compatibility, and version negotiation. TRIGGER when: designing, changing, or discussing any external API, working with .baboon model files, updating data models, adding new fields or types, bumping model versions, running code generation, implementing conversions, writing database migrations, or discussing API versioning strategy.
- constructive-test-taxonomy: Multi-axis classification of tests (Intention × Encapsulation × Isolation) for evaluating test quality and steering test-writing decisions. Replaces fuzzy Unit/Integration/E2E vocabulary with precise tags and a maintenance-cost heuristic. TRIGGER when: writing new tests, reviewing a test suite, deciding what to test or at what level, discussing mocks vs. real dependencies, classifying tests, or arguing about test coverage or test-strategy tradeoffs.
- dual-tests: Pattern for testing code that touches external systems: write one abstract test suite against an interface, then run it against both the production adapter (real DB/API/FS) and a hand-written in-memory dummy. Prefers manual dummies over auto-generated mocks. TRIGGER when: designing code that depends on a database, HTTP API, message queue, filesystem, or other external system; choosing between mocks, fakes, or dummies; adding integration points; setting up test infrastructure; or when the `constructive-test-taxonomy` skill calls for moving Communication-tier tests toward Blackbox-Atomic.
- environment: Bubblewrap "yolo" sandbox escape workflow (exchange scripts for reaching $HOME, system paths, or files outside the sandbox) plus in-sandbox nix/direnv usage. TRIGGER only when $SMIND_SANDBOXED=1 — i.e. running inside the yolo sandbox and you need to reach something outside it. SKIP entirely when $SMIND_SANDBOXED is unset: you are unsandboxed, so write directly and ignore this skill.
- izumi: Best-practice playbook for the izumi Scala ecosystem — distage (DI), distage-framework (roles/RoleAppMain), distage-testkit (test harness with memoization), distage-framework-docker (test containers), logstage (structured logging), and BIO (bifunctor effect typeclasses). Covers idiomatic bindings, Activation/Axis switching, Lifecycle discipline, plugin layout, and how to apply the `dual-tests` pattern inside distage so one test body runs against both a dummy and a real-DB adapter. TRIGGER when: writing or reviewing Scala code that imports `izumi.distage.*`, `izumi.logstage.*`, `izumi.functional.bio.*`, `distage.*`, `logstage.*`; designing a new distage plugin or Role; wiring a testkit `SpecZIO`/`Spec2`/`Spec1`; configuring `ContainerDef`/`DockerSupportModule`; choosing between `StandardAxis.Repo` or `Scene` tags; or implementing the dual-tests pattern in a distage project. The canonical reference project is https://github.com/7mind/distage-example.
- resilient-ws-ui: Guidelines for building applications that maintain reliable WebSocket connections to a backend and surface connection health clearly to the user. Invoke ONLY when the user explicitly references this skill by name (e.g. "/resilient-ws-ui", "use the resilient-ws-ui skill"). Do NOT auto-invoke based on topic match.
- tass: Target/Actual State Separation (TASS) pattern for reliable state management in event-driven control systems. TRIGGER when: implementing device or entity state management, designing control systems, working with target vs actual state separation, or building event-driven systems with commanded/observed state.
- cq:advance: Advance the WHOLE flow one cycle — chain /cq:investigate:advance, /cq:plan:advance, /cq:implement:advance to quiescence, then report DRAINED / BLOCKED-ON-QUESTIONS / MIXED.
- cq:implement-review: Implement-flow adversarial per-task review rubric + stdout-JSON contract, as a portable promptTemplate for non-Claude harnesses (pi/Codex). Symmetric to cq:plan-review. Judges ONE task's implementation, splits findings into autonomously-fixable criticism[], user-only questions[], and out-of-scope defects[], and emits the verdict as JSON on stdout. Writes NOTHING to the ledger — the orchestrator records the terminal review.
- cq:implement:advance: Advance an implement-flow run one full pass — pick DAG-ready tasks, implement each in an isolated worktree, adversarially review, autonomously fix criticism, register user questions, and merge back in dependency order.
- cq:implement:start: Start an implement-flow run — resolve the target milestones (default: all open ones), validate their task DAG, then hand off to the /cq:implement:advance loop.
- cq:investigate: Start an investigate-flow run — defect intake + bootstrap, then run the /cq:investigate:advance pass inline.
- cq:investigate:advance: Advance an investigate-flow run one research round — read defect state, form/extend the hypothesis tree, dispatch read-only explorers, validate every citation against source, adjudicate node status, and on a confirmed root cause file-and-defer the fix to plan-flow.
- cq:plan: Start a plan-flow goal — create the goal (from free text OR one-or-more idea-ids, one goal per idea), then hand off to the planner for the first clarifying questions.
- cq:plan-review: Shared adversarial plan-review rubric — judge an emitted plan for a goal against the fine-grained / sequenced / testable / grounded / complete rubric, classify findings into new_questions / criticism / defects, and emit a single fenced-json verdict on stdout. The CANONICAL rubric source; Claude's plan-reviewer agent and every non-Claude (Codex/Pi) reviewer judge the same way and emit the same JSON.
- cq:plan:advance: Advance plan-flow goals one full round — a given goal, or (no argument) every unlocked goal — running the planner↔reviewer loop until each needs the user or reaches `planned`.
- cq:plan:follow-up: Add new scope to an EXISTING plan-flow goal — append the follow-up request, re-open the goal, and hand to the planner for a fresh clarifying round.
- cq:planners: Session-only planner-set override — parse a natural-language planner instruction (e.g. "use grok and opus only") into canonical harness:model tokens via the cq.toml [aliases] table (from the ledger MCP get_planners/get_config) plus a documented fallback map; echo the resolved active set; confirm the override is SESSION-ONLY and reverts on the next fresh run.
- cq:reviewers: Session-only reviewer-set override — parse a natural-language reviewer instruction (e.g. "use grok and opus only") into canonical harness:model tokens via the cq.toml [aliases] table (from the ledger MCP get_reviewers/get_config) plus a documented fallback map; echo the resolved active set; confirm the override is SESSION-ONLY and reverts on the next fresh run.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- codex:rescue: Delegate investigation, an explicit fix request, or follow-up rescue work to the Codex rescue subagent
- codex:setup: Check whether the local Codex CLI is ready and optionally toggle the stop-time review gate
- codex:codex-cli-runtime: Internal helper contract for calling the codex-companion runtime from Claude Code
- codex:codex-result-handling: Internal guidance for presenting Codex helper output back to the user
- codex:gpt-5-4-prompting: Internal guidance for composing Codex and GPT-5.4 prompts for coding, review, diagnosis, and research tasks inside the Codex Claude Code plugin
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

1	package metrics
2	
3	import (
4		"github.com/prometheus/client_golang/prometheus"
5	
6		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/telemetry"
7	)
8	
9	// namespace prefixes every wanbond metric name; pathSubsystem and fecSubsystem
10	// partition the per-path and (future) FEC series.
11	const (
12		namespace     = "wanbond"
13		pathSubsystem = "path"
14		fecSubsystem  = "fec"
15	
16		// labelPath is the single label carried by every per-path series; its value is
17		// the stable path name from configuration (e.g. "starlink").
18		labelPath = "path"
19	)
20	
21	// Per-path metric names, exported so tests and future e2e harnesses can assert
22	// series by name without restating the FQ-name construction.
23	const (
24		MetricTxBytes    = "wanbond_path_tx_bytes_total"
25		MetricRxBytes    = "wanbond_path_rx_bytes_total"
26		MetricLoss       = "wanbond_path_loss_ratio"
27		MetricRTT        = "wanbond_path_rtt_seconds"
28		MetricJitter     = "wanbond_path_jitter_seconds"
29		MetricThroughput = "wanbond_path_throughput_bits_per_second"
30		MetricUp         = "wanbond_path_up"
31	)
32	
33	// FEC metric names. These connection-scoped series (no path label — FEC
34	// repair/recovery is per-connection, not per-uplink) are populated from the live FEC
35	// plane (T24): repair = parity frames emitted (the fixed-ratio overhead), recovered =
36	// data frames reconstructed from parity, unrecoverable = data frames lost beyond
37	// repair capacity.
38	const (
39		MetricFECData          = "wanbond_fec_data_packets_total"
40		MetricFECRepair        = "wanbond_fec_repair_packets_total"
41		MetricFECRecovered     = "wanbond_fec_recovered_packets_total"
42		MetricFECUnrecoverable = "wanbond_fec_unrecoverable_packets_total"
43		// Byte-denominated FEC overhead (T29). The adaptive-vs-fixed overhead comparison the
44		// P4 acceptance makes is in BYTES (parity shards are max-shard-sized while DATA frames
45		// vary), so these expose the byte numerator/denominator the frame counters above cannot:
46		// overhead = repair_bytes / data_bytes.
47		MetricFECDataBytes   = "wanbond_fec_data_bytes_total"
48		MetricFECRepairBytes = "wanbond_fec_repair_bytes_total"
49		// MetricFECResidualLoss is the post-FEC-recovery connection loss fraction (T29): the
50		// share of outer-seqs neither natively received nor reconstructed from parity — the loss
51		// FEC did not mask. It is the P4 residual-loss acceptance signal.
52		MetricFECResidualLoss = "wanbond_fec_residual_loss_ratio"
53	)
54	
55	// FECSnapshot is the current connection-scoped FEC signal set the exposition layer
56	// reports (T24). It is sourced from the multipath Bind's FEC counters, read at scrape
57	// time like the per-path snapshots. All zero when FEC is disabled.
58	type FECSnapshot struct {
59		// DataPackets is the cumulative count of DATA frames the FEC encoder emitted — the
60		// denominator of the fixed-ratio overhead (RepairPackets/DataPackets tends to M/K).
61		DataPackets uint64
62		// RepairPackets is the cumulative count of parity frames emitted — the fixed-ratio
63		// FEC overhead in packets.
64		RepairPackets uint64
65		// RecoveredPackets is the cumulative count of data frames reconstructed from parity.
66		RecoveredPackets uint64
67		// UnrecoverablePackets is the cumulative count of data frames lost beyond FEC repair
68		// capacity.
69		UnrecoverablePackets uint64
70		// DataBytes / RepairBytes are the cumulative DATA and parity frame WIRE bytes — the
71		// byte-denominated overhead numerator/denominator (T29): overhead = RepairBytes/DataBytes.
72		DataBytes   uint64
73		RepairBytes uint64
74		// ResidualLossRatio is the current post-FEC-recovery connection loss fraction in [0,1]
75		// (T29) — the loss FEC did not mask (the P4 acceptance signal). Zero when FEC is off.
76		ResidualLossRatio float64
77	}
78	
79	// PathSnapshot is the current per-path signal set the exposition layer reports.
80	// It fuses traffic accounting (TxBytes/RxBytes/Throughput, sourced from the bind
81	// and scheduler) with the telemetry plane's quality estimate and liveness verdict
82	// (Estimate/State, sourced verbatim from a Prober's Estimate()/State()). The
83	// metrics layer never measures these itself; it reads a Source at scrape time.
84	type PathSnapshot struct {
85		// Name is the stable path identifier used as the `path` label. It must be
86		// unique within a single Source.Paths() result.
87		Name string
88		// TxBytes and RxBytes are cumulative byte counters for the path.
89		TxBytes uint64
90		RxBytes uint64
91		// ThroughputBitsPerSecond is the path's current send+receive throughput.
92		ThroughputBitsPerSecond float64
93		// Estimate carries per-path RTT/jitter/loss, read verbatim from telemetry.
94		Estimate telemetry.Estimate
95		// State is the per-path liveness verdict, read verbatim from telemetry.
96		State telemetry.PathState
97	}
98	
99	// Source is the read-only seam between the traffic/telemetry planes and the
100	// exposition layer. The collector calls Paths at every scrape, so an
101	// implementation must be safe for concurrent use and must return a consistent
102	// snapshot (unique path names) cheaply — it is on the scrape hot path.
103	type Source interface {
104		// Paths returns the current per-path snapshots.
105		Paths() []PathSnapshot
106		// FEC returns the current connection-scoped FEC counters (T24).
107		FEC() FECSnapshot
108	}
109	
110	// collector is a prometheus.Collector that reads a Source at scrape time and
111	// emits per-path const-metrics plus the placeholder FEC counters. Reading at
112	// scrape time (rather than mirroring into GaugeVecs on an update path) keeps the
113	// exposition consistent with the live telemetry with no duplicated state and no
114	// staleness window.
115	type collector struct {
116		src Source
117	
118		txBytes    *prometheus.Desc
119		rxBytes    *prometheus.Desc
120		loss       *prometheus.Desc
121		rtt        *prometheus.Desc
122		jitter     *prometheus.Desc
123		throughput *prometheus.Desc
124		up         *prometheus.Desc
125	
126		fecData          *prometheus.Desc
127		fecRepair        *prometheus.Desc
128		fecRecovered     *prometheus.Desc
129		fecUnrecoverable *prometheus.Desc
130		fecDataBytes     *prometheus.Desc
131		fecRepairBytes   *prometheus.Desc
132		fecResidualLoss  *prometheus.Desc
133	}
134	
135	// NewCollector builds the wanbond metrics collector over src. Register it into a
136	// dedicated prometheus.Registry (see NewServer); it deliberately does not touch
137	// the global default registry (no-globals discipline).
138	func NewCollector(src Source) prometheus.Collector {
139		pathLabels := []string{labelPath}
140		desc := func(subsystem, name, help string, labels []string) *prometheus.Desc {
141			return prometheus.NewDesc(prometheus.BuildFQName(namespace, subsystem, name), help, labels, nil)
142		}
143		return &collector{
144			src:        src,
145			txBytes:    desc(pathSubsystem, "tx_bytes_total", "Total bytes transmitted on the path.", pathLabels),
146			rxBytes:    desc(pathSubsystem, "rx_bytes_total", "Total bytes received on the path.", pathLabels),
147			loss:       desc(pathSubsystem, "loss_ratio", "Per-path probe loss fraction in [0,1].", pathLabels),
148			rtt:        desc(pathSubsystem, "rtt_seconds", "Smoothed per-path round-trip time in seconds.", pathLabels),
149			jitter:     desc(pathSubsystem, "jitter_seconds", "Smoothed per-path RTT deviation (jitter) in seconds.", pathLabels),
150			throughput: desc(pathSubsystem, "throughput_bits_per_second", "Current per-path throughput in bits per second.", pathLabels),
151			up:         desc(pathSubsystem, "up", "Per-path liveness (1 = up, 0 = down).", pathLabels),
152	
153			fecData:          desc(fecSubsystem, "data_packets_total", "FEC DATA packets emitted (the fixed-ratio overhead denominator).", nil),
154			fecRepair:        desc(fecSubsystem, "repair_packets_total", "FEC parity packets emitted (the fixed-ratio overhead).", nil),
155			fecRecovered:     desc(fecSubsystem, "recovered_packets_total", "Data packets reconstructed via FEC.", nil),
156			fecUnrecoverable: desc(fecSubsystem, "unrecoverable_packets_total", "Data packets lost beyond FEC repair capacity.", nil),
157			fecDataBytes:     desc(fecSubsystem, "data_bytes_total", "FEC DATA-frame wire bytes emitted (the byte overhead denominator).", nil),
158			fecRepairBytes:   desc(fecSubsystem, "repair_bytes_total", "FEC parity-frame wire bytes emitted (the byte overhead numerator).", nil),
159			fecResidualLoss:  desc(fecSubsystem, "residual_loss_ratio", "Post-FEC-recovery connection loss fraction in [0,1] (loss FEC did not mask).", nil),
160		}
161	}
162	
163	// Describe sends every descriptor; the collector's series set is fixed even
164	// though its per-path label values are discovered at Collect time.
165	func (c *collector) Describe(ch chan<- *prometheus.Desc) {
166		ch <- c.txBytes
167		ch <- c.rxBytes
168		ch <- c.loss
169		ch <- c.rtt
170		ch <- c.jitter
171		ch <- c.throughput
172		ch <- c.up
173		ch <- c.fecData
174		ch <- c.fecRepair
175		ch <- c.fecRecovered
176		ch <- c.fecUnrecoverable
177		ch <- c.fecDataBytes
178		ch <- c.fecRepairBytes
179		ch <- c.fecResidualLoss
180	}
181	
182	// Collect reads the Source once and emits one const-metric per per-path series,
183	// then the three connection-scoped FEC counters read from the live FEC plane.
184	func (c *collector) Collect(ch chan<- prometheus.Metric) {
185		for _, p := range c.src.Paths() {
186			ch <- prometheus.MustNewConstMetric(c.txBytes, prometheus.CounterValue, float64(p.TxBytes), p.Name)
187			ch <- prometheus.MustNewConstMetric(c.rxBytes, prometheus.CounterValue, float64(p.RxBytes), p.Name)
188			ch <- prometheus.MustNewConstMetric(c.loss, prometheus.GaugeValue, p.Estimate.Loss, p.Name)
189			ch <- prometheus.MustNewConstMetric(c.rtt, prometheus.GaugeValue, p.Estimate.RTT.Seconds(), p.Name)
190			ch <- prometheus.MustNewConstMetric(c.jitter, prometheus.GaugeValue, p.Estimate.Jitter.Seconds(), p.Name)
191			ch <- prometheus.MustNewConstMetric(c.throughput, prometheus.GaugeValue, p.ThroughputBitsPerSecond, p.Name)
192			ch <- prometheus.MustNewConstMetric(c.up, prometheus.GaugeValue, upValue(p.State), p.Name)
193		}
194		f := c.src.FEC()

197		ch <- prometheus.MustNewConstMetric(c.fecRecovered, prometheus.CounterValue, float64(f.RecoveredPackets))

200		ch <- prometheus.MustNewConstMetric(c.fecRepairBytes, prometheus.CounterValue, float64(f.RepairBytes))
201		ch <- prometheus.MustNewConstMetric(c.fecResidualLoss, prometheus.GaugeValue, f.ResidualLossRatio)
202	}

204	// upValue maps a liveness verdict to the wanbond_path_up gauge value.
205	func upValue(s telemetry.PathState) float64 {
206		if s == telemetry.StateUp {
207			return 1
208		}
209		return 0
210	}

1	package device

3	import (
4		"sync"
5	
6		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/bind"
7		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/metrics"
8		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/telemetry"
9	)
10	
11	// trafficProvider is the read seam the metrics adapter consumes: the multipath Bind's
12	// per-path traffic+telemetry snapshot (T23) and its connection-scoped FEC counters
13	// (T24). *bind.Multipath satisfies it; a fake satisfies it in the adapter's unit test,
14	// so the mapping and rate derivation are testable without a running engine.
15	type trafficProvider interface {
16		PathSnapshots() []bind.PathTraffic
17		FECSnapshot() bind.FECStats
18	}
19	
20	// metricsSource adapts the multipath Bind onto metrics.Source: at every scrape it
21	// reads the Bind's cumulative per-path byte counters + telemetry, and DERIVES the
22	// per-path throughput as the rate of the (tx+rx) byte-counter delta since the previous
23	// scrape. Deriving the rate here (rather than in the Bind) keeps the Bind's hot path a
24	// pair of lock-free atomic Adds with no timekeeping, and confines the only stateful,
25	// scrape-cadence-dependent piece to this adapter. The first scrape of a path reports
26	// zero throughput (no prior sample); a counter that appears to move backwards (only
27	// possible across a Close→Open counter reset) also yields zero for that interval rather
28	// than a spurious negative.
29	type metricsSource struct {
30		provider trafficProvider
31		clock    telemetry.Clock
32	
33		mu   sync.Mutex
34		last map[string]byteSample
35	}
36	
37	// byteSample is the previous scrape's cumulative (tx+rx) total for one path and the
38	// instant it was taken, so the next scrape can divide the delta by the elapsed time.
39	type byteSample struct {
40		total   uint64
41		atNanos int64
42	}
43	
44	// newMetricsSource builds a metrics.Source over the Bind. The clock is injected so the
45	// throughput derivation is deterministic under test.
46	func newMetricsSource(provider trafficProvider, clock telemetry.Clock) *metricsSource {
47		return &metricsSource{provider: provider, clock: clock, last: make(map[string]byteSample)}
48	}
49	
50	// Paths implements metrics.Source. It is called on the scrape goroutine (the /metrics
51	// HTTP handler), which the prometheus registry may run concurrently, so the per-path
52	// last-sample map is guarded. The underlying provider.PathSnapshots() reads atomics and
53	// the prober's synchronized Estimate without holding the Bind's send lock across a
54	// prober call, so scraping never blocks Send.
55	func (s *metricsSource) Paths() []metrics.PathSnapshot {
56		tr := s.provider.PathSnapshots()
57		nowNanos := s.clock.Now().UnixNano()
58	
59		s.mu.Lock()
60		defer s.mu.Unlock()
61		out := make([]metrics.PathSnapshot, len(tr))
62		for i, t := range tr {
63			total := t.TxBytes + t.RxBytes
64			var tput float64
65			if prev, ok := s.last[t.Name]; ok {
66				dtSeconds := float64(nowNanos-prev.atNanos) / 1e9
67				if dtSeconds > 0 && total >= prev.total {
68					tput = float64(total-prev.total) * 8 / dtSeconds
69				}
70			}
71			s.last[t.Name] = byteSample{total: total, atNanos: nowNanos}
72			out[i] = metrics.PathSnapshot{
73				Name:                    t.Name,
74				TxBytes:                 t.TxBytes,
75				RxBytes:                 t.RxBytes,
76				ThroughputBitsPerSecond: tput,
77				Estimate:                t.Estimate,
78				State:                   t.State,
79			}
80		}
81		return out
82	}
83	
84	// FEC implements metrics.Source: it reads the Bind's connection-scoped FEC counters
85	// (T24) verbatim at scrape time. Unlike the per-path throughput, the FEC counters are
86	// cumulative and need no rate derivation or prior-sample state, so this is a direct
87	// pass-through of the Bind's lock-free snapshot.
88	func (s *metricsSource) FEC() metrics.FECSnapshot {
89		f := s.provider.FECSnapshot()
90		return metrics.FECSnapshot{
91			DataPackets:          f.DataFrames,
92			RepairPackets:        f.ParityFrames,
93			RecoveredPackets:     f.Recovered,
94			UnrecoverablePackets: f.Unrecoverable,
95			DataBytes:            f.DataBytes,
96			RepairBytes:          f.ParityBytes,
97			ResidualLossRatio:    f.ResidualLoss,
98		}
99	}

1	// Package device brings a wanbond tunnel up from a validated configuration: it
2	// creates the TUN interface, drives the embedded amneziawg-go engine over the
3	// multipath Bind, and applies the WireGuard (and, when configured, amnezia
4	// obfuscation) parameters via the engine's UAPI. It owns ONLY the tunnel engine
5	// — interface addressing and routing are left to the operator (systemd/wg-quick
6	// style), so the daemon stays free of privileged shell-outs. The interface name
7	// is exposed via Tunnel.Name for that external configuration step.
8	package device
9	
10	import (
11		"context"
12		"crypto/rand"
13		"encoding/hex"
14		"fmt"
15		"net/netip"
16		"reflect"
17		"strings"
18		"sync"
19		"time"
20	
21		awgdevice "github.com/amnezia-vpn/amneziawg-go/device"
22		"github.com/amnezia-vpn/amneziawg-go/tun"
23	
24		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/adaptivefec"
25		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/bind"
26		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/config"
27		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/dnsresolve"
28		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/fec"
29		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/log"
30		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/metrics"
31		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/sched"
32		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/telemetry"
33	)
34	
35	// metricsShutdownTimeout bounds the graceful shutdown of the /metrics endpoint on
36	// Close (and on a reload that rebinds it). The endpoint is loopback-only with no
37	// long-lived scrapes, so this is comfortably generous.
38	const metricsShutdownTimeout = 2 * time.Second
39	
40	// defaultTUNName is the requested interface name; the kernel honours it unless it
41	// collides (it never does across the edge and concentrator network namespaces).
42	const defaultTUNName = "wanbond0"
43	
44	// tunMTU is the tunnel (TUN) MTU for a config: the default path MTU minus the
45	// IP+UDP, outer DATA-frame, and WireGuard transport overheads, so a full-MTU inner
46	// packet never fragments on the wire (see internal/bind mtu.go and docs/p1-mtu.md).
47	// This is smaller than the plain-WireGuard 1420 because the bonding layer adds its
48	// own outer DATA frame per datagram; with FEC enabled it is a further
49	// bind.FECParityMTUPenalty smaller so a full-size PARITY frame also fits the path MTU
50	// (T24) rather than fragmenting.
51	func tunMTU(cfg *config.Config) int {
52		return bind.InnerMTU(bind.DefaultPathMTU, cfg.FEC.Enabled)
53	}
54	
55	// Tunnel is a running wanbond tunnel: the amneziawg engine, its TUN, and the
56	// multipath Bind. Close tears all three down.
57	type Tunnel struct {
58		dev  *awgdevice.Device
59		tun  tun.Device
60		name string
61		// bind is the multipath Bind the engine drives; Reload calls its runtime
62		// AddPath/RemovePath to apply a config-reload path diff (T30).
63		bind *bind.Multipath
64		// cfg is the configuration the RUNNING tunnel reflects: the boot config, its path
65		// set updated on each successful Reload to the current membership (survivors keep
66		// their original parameters — a membership-only reload does not re-apply modified
67		// path parameters or non-path fields). Reload diffs the next desired config against
68		// it to warn about changes it cannot apply. Guarded by reloadMu.
69		cfg *config.Config
70		// reloadMu serializes Reload against itself (SIGHUP handlers), guarding cfg.
71		reloadMu sync.Mutex
72		// log is the device-component logger, retained for Reload diagnostics.
73		log log.Logger
74		// stopProbes halts the per-path probe cadence loop (T37). Close calls it before
75		// tearing the engine down so emission stops before the sockets close. It is
76		// idempotent and never nil (a no-op when the bind runs without probers).
77		stopProbes func()
78		// stopReconcile halts the background deferred-path reconcile loop (T55): it retries
79		// binding the paths a tolerant Open left DOWN as their addresses appear. Close calls
80		// it before tearing the engine down so no reconcile races the socket close. It is
81		// idempotent and never nil (a no-op when the bind runs without probers / never defers).
82		stopReconcile func()
83		// stopHubFailover halts the edge-side hub-failover monitor loop (T57): it watches the
84		// per-path liveness plane and, on hub loss (all paths to the active concentrator DOWN),
85		// advances to the next ordered peer endpoint, repoints the bond's remote, and initiates
86		// a fresh WG re-handshake. Close calls it before tearing the engine down. It is
87		// idempotent and never nil (a no-op on the concentrator, or an edge with a single
88		// concentrator endpoint, or a bind without the probe transport).
89		stopHubFailover func()
90		// stopResolution halts the edge-side DNS re-resolution loop (T74): it re-resolves each
91		// opted-in hostname peer-endpoint spec on the [dns] poll cadence and out-of-band on hub loss,
92		// feeding fresh records to the hub-failover controller. Close calls it between stopHubFailover
93		// and dev.Close. It is idempotent and never nil (a no-op on the concentrator, an all-literal
94		// edge, or when no hostname spec is configured — Q29 inertness).
95		stopResolution func()
96		// amnezia is the obfuscation profile this tunnel holds against the
97		// process-global amnezia guard (see amneziaGuard); Close releases it.
98		amnezia     config.Amnezia
99		releaseOnce sync.Once
100	
101		// metricsSrc is the live metrics.Source over the Bind; it is stable for the tunnel's
102		// life (the Bind pointer never changes), so a reload that rebinds the endpoint reuses
103		// the SAME Source — its derived-throughput last-sample state survives the rebind.
104		metricsSrc metrics.Source
105		// metricsSrv is the running /metrics endpoint, nil when [metrics].listen is empty.
106		// It is (re)assigned by applyMetricsLocked and read by Close; both hold reloadMu, so
107		// a SIGHUP-driven rebind never races shutdown. metricsListen mirrors the address it
108		// is bound to so a reload can detect a listen change without inspecting the server.
109		metricsSrv    *metrics.Server
110		metricsListen string
111	}
112	
113	// SINGLE-ENGINE-PER-PROCESS INVARIANT (defect D2).
114	//
115	// amneziawg-go stores the amnezia magic-header message types in PACKAGE-GLOBAL
116	// variables — device.MessageInitiationType, MessageResponseType,
117	// MessageCookieReplyType, MessageTransportType. (*device.Device).IpcSet assigns
118	// them (in handlePostConfig) from a configured engine's profile, and
119	// (*device.Device).Close restores them to the WireGuard defaults (in
120	// resetProtocol) UNCONDITIONALLY — closing ANY engine, plain or configured,
121	// reverts the process-global message types to their defaults.
122	//
123	// Two consequences make a CONFIGURED (amnezia) engine require PROCESS
124	// EXCLUSIVITY:
125	//   - a second configured engine would overwrite the first engine's message-type
126	//     framing at IpcSet; and even with the SAME profile, closing the first engine
127	//     runs resetProtocol and reverts the globals to defaults under the second,
128	//     still-live engine, silently dropping its tunnel to plain-WireGuard framing.
129	//   - closing ANY other engine — a PLAIN (unconfigured) one included — runs
130	//     resetProtocol and resets the globals out from under a live configured engine.
131	//
132	// So the rule wanbond ASSERTS (rather than vendor-patching the fork) is:
133	//   - a configured engine may come up only when NO other engine is live, and
134	//   - no engine (plain or configured) may come up while a configured engine is live.
135	//
136	// PLAIN engines may coexist with each other: they never set the globals, and
137	// resetProtocol on their Close only restores the defaults they already use, so it
138	// is idempotent among them. wanbond runs exactly one tunnel per process, so the
139	// guard only ever trips on genuine misuse.
140	type amneziaGuard struct {
141		mu         sync.Mutex
142		plainLive  int  // number of live plain-WireGuard (unconfigured) engines
143		configLive bool // whether a configured (amnezia) engine is live (at most one)
144	}
145	
146	// globalAmneziaGuard enforces the single-amnezia-engine-per-process invariant for
147	// every Tunnel brought up in this process.
148	var globalAmneziaGuard amneziaGuard
149	
150	// acquire registers an about-to-start engine against the process-exclusivity rule
151	// (see amneziaGuard). A configured (amnezia) engine is admitted only when NO other
152	// engine is live. A plain engine is admitted only when no configured engine is
153	// live; plain engines may coexist with one another. The caller must release the
154	// SAME profile exactly once when the engine is torn down (plain engines included —
155	// release is no longer a no-op for them).
156	func (g *amneziaGuard) acquire(a config.Amnezia) error {
157		g.mu.Lock()
158		defer g.mu.Unlock()
159		if a.Configured() {
160			if g.configLive || g.plainLive > 0 {
161				return fmt.Errorf("device: refusing to start an amnezia-configured engine while another engine is live: " +
162					"amneziawg-go keeps the magic-header message types in process-global state and resets them to defaults on ANY engine's Close, " +
163					"so a configured engine requires process exclusivity — at most one, with no other engine alongside it (D2)")
164			}
165			g.configLive = true
166			return nil
167		}
168		if g.configLive {
169			return fmt.Errorf("device: refusing to start a second engine while an amnezia-configured engine is live: " +
170				"closing this engine would reset amneziawg-go's process-global message types to defaults under the live amnezia tunnel (D2)")
171		}
172		g.plainLive++
173		return nil
174	}
175	
176	// release drops an engine's hold on the guard. A configured engine clears the
177	// single configured slot; a plain engine decrements the live plain count.
178	func (g *amneziaGuard) release(a config.Amnezia) {
179		g.mu.Lock()
180		defer g.mu.Unlock()
181		if a.Configured() {
182			g.configLive = false
183			return
184		}
185		if g.plainLive > 0 {
186			g.plainLive--
187		}
188	}
189	
190	// Up creates the TUN, wires the multipath Bind into the amneziawg engine,
191	// applies the crypto configuration from cfg, and brings the device up. The same
192	// path drives both roles; the role only changes which UAPI fields cfg carries
193	// (the concentrator sets listen_port; the edge sets each peer's endpoint).
194	func Up(cfg *config.Config, lg log.Logger) (*Tunnel, error) {
195		clg := lg.Component("device")
196	
197		tunDev, err := tun.CreateTUN(defaultTUNName, tunMTU(cfg))
198		if err != nil {
199			return nil, fmt.Errorf("device: create TUN: %w", err)
200		}
201		name, err := tunDev.Name()
202		if err != nil {
203			_ = tunDev.Close()
204			return nil, fmt.Errorf("device: read TUN name: %w", err)
205		}
206		// Bring the interface administratively UP now (I1): amneziawg-go never does this
207		// itself, and a write to a DOWN TUN yields EIO — a silent-dead-tunnel footgun the
208		// operator previously had to work around with an out-of-band `ip link set up`.
209		// Addressing stays operator-owned; this sets ONLY the IFF_UP flag (see ifUp).
210		if err := ifUp(name); err != nil {
211			_ = tunDev.Close()
212			return nil, fmt.Errorf("device: bring interface up: %w", err)
213		}
214		clg.Info("interface up", "interface", name)
215		// The DNS re-resolution transport is the one the (validated) [dns] block selects. It is passed
216		// as a factory (not eagerly constructed) so up() builds it AT MOST ONCE and ONLY when some peer
217		// carries a hostname endpoint spec — a config with zero hostname specs never constructs a
218		// resolver (Q29 inertness). The injected TUN and factory are also the seams device tests drive.
219		return up(cfg, clg, tunDev, name, cfg.DNS.NewResolver)
220	}
221	
222	// resolverFactory builds the DNS resolver used for hostname endpoint re-resolution. It is injected
223	// so a device test can supply a fake; production passes cfg.DNS.NewResolver. up() invokes it AT
224	// MOST ONCE, and ONLY when some peer carries a hostname endpoint spec.
225	type resolverFactory func() (dnsresolve.Resolver, error)
226	
227	// up drives the tunnel bring-up over an already-created TUN and an injected resolver factory: it
228	// wires the multipath Bind into the amneziawg engine, performs the bounded initial hostname
229	// resolve (Q30), applies the crypto/endpoint UAPI config built ONLY from resolved entries, brings
230	// the device up, and starts the probe / reconcile / hub-failover / re-resolution loops. Splitting
231	// it out of Up gives device tests a seam to inject a channel TUN and a fake resolver without the
232	// privileged tun.CreateTUN. The same path drives both roles; the role only changes which UAPI
233	// fields cfg carries (the concentrator sets listen_port; the edge sets each peer's endpoint).
234	func up(cfg *config.Config, clg log.Logger, tunDev tun.Device, name string, newResolver resolverFactory) (*Tunnel, error) {
235		// Claim the single-amnezia-engine-per-process invariant (D2) BEFORE IpcSet
236		// assigns amneziawg-go's process-global message-type state. On any failure
237		// below, ok stays false and the deferred release returns the hold; the
238		// successful path transfers the hold to the returned Tunnel.
239		if err := globalAmneziaGuard.acquire(cfg.Amnezia); err != nil {
240			_ = tunDev.Close()
241			return nil, err
242		}
243		ok := false
244		defer func() {
245			if !ok {
246				globalAmneziaGuard.release(cfg.Amnezia)
247			}
248		}()
249	
250		// One live *telemetry.Prober per path drives on-wire liveness. The SAME prober
251		// values back the scheduler's PathHealth AND the bind's probe transport, so the
252		// liveness the probe loop measures is exactly the liveness the scheduler selects
253		// on (T37 replaces the sched.AlwaysUp placeholder here).
254		scheduler, probers, newProber, err := buildScheduler(cfg, clg)
255		if err != nil {
256			_ = tunDev.Close()
257			return nil, fmt.Errorf("device: build scheduler: %w", err)
258		}
259		mpBind, err := bind.NewMultipath(cfg.Paths, cfg.PSK, scheduler, probers, newProber, fecConfig(cfg.FEC), adaptiveFECConfig(cfg.FEC), cfg.Amnezia)
260		if err != nil {
261			_ = tunDev.Close()
262			return nil, fmt.Errorf("device: build multipath bind: %w", err)
263		}
264		dev := awgdevice.NewDevice(tunDev, mpBind, engineLogger(clg, cfg.Log.Level))
265	
266		// Bounded initial hostname resolution (Q30): construct the resolver ONCE (only when some peer
267		// carries a hostname spec — Q29 inertness), resolve each hostname spec under a short timeout,
268		// and seed its expansion. The per-peer boot endpoint — the flattened head of the seeded specs —
269		// is what the UAPI render installs on the engine peer; a hostname that does not resolve in the
270		// boot window (or a resolver that fails to construct) leaves its spec EMPTY and the peer boots
271		// WITHOUT a peer endpoint (tolerant boot). The re-resolution loop then completes the wiring on
272		// first success via the FIRST-RESOLVE INSTALL PATH (R70). Literal specs are seeded verbatim, so
273		// an all-literal peer boots byte-for-byte as before.
274		boot := resolveBootEndpoints(cfg, newResolver, clg)
275	
276		uapi, err := uapiConfig(cfg, boot.endpoints)
277		if err != nil {
278			dev.Close()
279			return nil, fmt.Errorf("device: build UAPI config: %w", err)
280		}
281		if err := dev.IpcSet(uapi); err != nil {
282			dev.Close()
283			return nil, fmt.Errorf("device: apply UAPI config: %w", err)
284		}
285		if err := dev.Up(); err != nil {
286			dev.Close()
287			return nil, fmt.Errorf("device: bring up: %w", err)
288		}
289	
290		// The engine has opened the bind (dev.Up → BindUpdate → Open), so the per-path
291		// sockets exist: start the probe cadence now. Close stops it before dev.Close.
292		// The interval is the SINGLE-SOURCE-OF-TRUTH telemetry default, which also arms
293		// the bind's receive-path liveness sweep throttle (D15).
294		stopProbes := mpBind.StartProbeLoop(telemetry.DefaultProbeInterval)
295		// Start the background deferred-path reconciler alongside probing (T55): a path the
296		// tolerant Open left DOWN (its source_addr not yet assignable — a 5G modem without a
297		// lease at boot) is retried and promoted to a live path as its address appears,
298		// WITHOUT a tunnel restart. Close stops it before dev.Close, like the probe loop.
299		stopReconcile := mpBind.StartReconcileLoop(bind.DefaultReconcileInterval)
300		// Start the edge-side hub-failover monitor (T57) AND the DNS re-resolution loop (T74): on hub
301		// loss (every path to the active concentrator DOWN) failover advances to the next ordered peer
302		// endpoint, repoints the bond's remote, and re-handshakes; re-resolution re-resolves opted-in
303		// hostname specs and installs/repoints on change. Both a no-op on the concentrator, a
304		// single-IP-literal edge, or a bind without the probe transport. Started AFTER dev.Up so the
305		// engine peer the re-handshake / endpoint-install look up exists (IpcSet added it above). Close
306		// stops both before dev.Close, like the probe/reconcile loops.
307		stopHubFailover, stopResolution := startFailoverAndResolution(cfg, mpBind, probers, dev, boot, clg)
308	
309		t := &Tunnel{
310			dev: dev, tun: tunDev, name: name, bind: mpBind, cfg: cfg, log: clg,
311			stopProbes: stopProbes, stopReconcile: stopReconcile,
312			stopHubFailover: stopHubFailover, stopResolution: stopResolution, amnezia: cfg.Amnezia,
313			// The Source reads live per-path counters/telemetry from the Bind and derives
314			// throughput from the byte-counter delta between scrapes (see metricsSource). It is
315			// built unconditionally (cheap) so a reload that later turns [metrics].listen ON has
316			// a Source ready; the endpoint itself is started only when a listen is configured.
317			metricsSrc: newMetricsSource(mpBind, telemetry.SystemClock{}),
318		}
319	
320		// Stand up the /metrics endpoint when configured. A non-loopback listen is refused
321		// by metrics.NewServer (fail fast) — surface it as an Up failure rather than booting
322		// a tunnel that silently exposes per-path operational data off-host.
323		t.reloadMu.Lock()
324		err = t.applyMetricsLocked(cfg.Metrics.Listen)
325		t.reloadMu.Unlock()
326		if err != nil {
327			// The tunnel is fully constructed and holds the amnezia guard, so transfer that
328			// ownership to it (ok=true) BEFORE tearing it down: t.Close releases the guard via
329			// releaseOnce, and suppressing the !ok defer avoids a double release. t.Close also
330			// stops the probe loop and closes the engine/TUN.
331			ok = true
332			t.Close()
333			return nil, fmt.Errorf("device: start metrics endpoint: %w", err)
334		}
335	
336		ok = true
337		clg.Info("tunnel up", "interface", name, "role", string(cfg.Role))
338		return t, nil
339	}
340	
341	// applyMetricsLocked reconciles the running /metrics endpoint to listen: it starts the
342	// endpoint when one is desired and none runs, stops it when listen is empty, and rebinds
343	// (stop old, start new) when the address changed. It is idempotent for an unchanged
344	// address. The Source is reused across a rebind so its derived-throughput state is not
345	// reset. The caller MUST hold reloadMu (Up and Reload both do), which also serializes it
346	// against Close reading metricsSrv. On a NewServer/refuse error the previous server is
347	// left running untouched, so a bad reload never drops a working endpoint.
348	func (t *Tunnel) applyMetricsLocked(listen string) error {
349		if listen == t.metricsListen {
350			return nil
351		}
352		if listen == "" {
353			t.stopMetricsLocked()
354			return nil
355		}
356		srv, err := metrics.NewServer(listen, t.metricsSrc, t.log)
357		if err != nil {
358			return err
359		}
360		t.stopMetricsLocked()
361		srv.Start()
362		t.metricsSrv = srv
363		t.metricsListen = listen
364		return nil
365	}
366	
367	// stopMetricsLocked gracefully shuts the running endpoint down (if any) and clears the
368	// bookkeeping. Caller holds reloadMu.
369	func (t *Tunnel) stopMetricsLocked() {
370		if t.metricsSrv == nil {
371			return
372		}
373		ctx, cancel := context.WithTimeout(context.Background(), metricsShutdownTimeout)
374		defer cancel()
375		if err := t.metricsSrv.Close(ctx); err != nil {
376			t.log.Warn("metrics endpoint shutdown error", "error", err.Error())
377		}
378		t.metricsSrv = nil
379		t.metricsListen = ""
380	}
381	
382	// Reload applies a reloaded configuration to the RUNNING tunnel by diffing its
383	// path set against the live one and adding/removing paths at runtime (T30), WITHOUT
384	// tearing the tunnel down: the WG session, the surviving paths, and in-flight
385	// resequencing are all preserved. cfg is assumed already validated (config.Load
386	// fails fast on a bad file, so a bad reload never reaches here and never disturbs
387	// the running tunnel). Only the path set is diffed — the crypto/amnezia/role fields
388	// are fixed for the life of the process and a reload does not re-key the engine.
389	//
390	// New paths are added BEFORE absent ones are removed, so a failed addition aborts
391	// with every existing path still in service. Diffing is by name: a path present in
392	// both sets is left untouched (its source/dest parameters are not re-read).
393	//
394	// A membership-only reload CANNOT apply every kind of change: a same-name path whose
395	// source/dest changed, a path REORDER (index 0 is the preferred primary, so a reorder
396	// is a priority change), and every non-path field (psk, wireguard, amnezia, role, log,
397	// metrics) are all out of scope for T30. Rather than silently diverge from the file,
398	// Reload logs an EXPLICIT WARNING per ignored change (reloadWarnings) so the operator
399	// knows exactly what was dropped; only the path-membership add/remove is applied.
400	func (t *Tunnel) Reload(cfg *config.Config) error {
401		t.reloadMu.Lock()
402		defer t.reloadMu.Unlock()
403	
404		for _, w := range reloadWarnings(t.cfg, cfg) {
405			t.log.Warn("reload: ignored config change (path-membership reload only)", "change", w)
406		}
407	
408		// Rebind the /metrics endpoint if its listen address changed (T23). This is one of
409		// the few non-path fields a reload DOES apply: the endpoint is external to the WG
410		// session and the Bind, so restarting it disturbs neither. applyMetricsLocked reuses
411		// the tunnel's stable Source (its derived-throughput state survives), and on a refuse
412		// (e.g. a newly non-loopback address) leaves the previous endpoint running and fails
413		// the reload — the running tunnel is never disturbed. Applied BEFORE the path diff so
414		// a metrics-refuse aborts before any membership change.
415		if cfg.Metrics.Listen != t.metricsListen {
416			if err := t.applyMetricsLocked(cfg.Metrics.Listen); err != nil {
417				return fmt.Errorf("device: reload metrics endpoint: %w", err)
418			}
419			t.log.Info("reload: metrics endpoint rebound", "listen", cfg.Metrics.Listen)
420		}
421	
422		add, remove := diffPaths(t.bind.PathNames(), cfg.Paths)
423		for _, def := range add {
424			if err := t.bind.AddPath(def); err != nil {
425				return fmt.Errorf("device: reload add path %q: %w", def.Name, err)
426			}
427			t.log.Info("reload: path added", "path", def.Name)
428		}
429		for _, name := range remove {
430			if err := t.bind.RemovePath(name); err != nil {
431				return fmt.Errorf("device: reload remove path %q: %w", name, err)
432			}
433			t.log.Info("reload: path removed", "path", name)
434		}
435		// Advance the running config to the membership now in service. Survivors keep their
436		// ORIGINAL parameters and all non-path fields stay as booted (the ignored changes
437		// were not applied), so a subsequent identical reload re-warns about a still-diverged
438		// file rather than silently accepting it. Metrics.Listen is carried to the applied
439		// value so a subsequent reload diffs against the endpoint actually running.
440		t.cfg = runningConfig(t.cfg, add, remove)
441		t.cfg.Metrics.Listen = t.metricsListen
442		return nil
443	}
444	
445	// reloadWarnings compares the desired config against the currently-running one and
446	// returns one human-readable warning per change a path-membership-only reload (T30)
447	// cannot apply: a modified same-name path (source/dest), a path reorder, and any
448	// non-path field. Membership add/remove is NOT reported here — it is applied. Full
449	// modify-support is out of scope; SILENCE is not — the operator must be told what was
450	// ignored. It is a pure function so the warning set is unit-testable.
451	func reloadWarnings(live, desired *config.Config) []string {
452		var w []string
453	
454		if live.Role != desired.Role {
455			w = append(w, fmt.Sprintf("role %q -> %q", live.Role, desired.Role))
456		}
457		if !reflect.DeepEqual(live.PSK, desired.PSK) {
458			w = append(w, "psk changed")
459		}
460		if !reflect.DeepEqual(live.WireGuard, desired.WireGuard) {
461			w = append(w, "wireguard section changed")
462		}
463		if !reflect.DeepEqual(live.Amnezia, desired.Amnezia) {
464			w = append(w, "amnezia section changed")
465		}
466		if !reflect.DeepEqual(live.Log, desired.Log) {
467			w = append(w, "log section changed")
468		}
469		// NOTE: a Metrics change is NOT warned here — unlike the other non-path fields, the
470		// reload APPLIES it by rebinding the /metrics endpoint (see Reload). Warning about a
471		// change that is honoured would misinform the operator.
472	
473		// Same-name paths whose parameters changed: diffPaths matches by name only, so a
474		// modified source/dest on an existing path is otherwise silently dropped.
475		liveByName := make(map[string]config.Path, len(live.Paths))
476		for _, p := range live.Paths {
477			liveByName[p.Name] = p
478		}
479		for _, d := range desired.Paths {
480			l, ok := liveByName[d.Name]
481			if !ok {
482				continue // a genuinely new path is added, not ignored
483			}
484			if l.SourceAddr != d.SourceAddr || l.DestAddr != d.DestAddr {
485				w = append(w, fmt.Sprintf("path %q source/dest changed — the running path keeps its original binding", d.Name))
486			}
487		}
488	
489		if reordered(live.Paths, desired.Paths) {
490			w = append(w, "path priority order changed (reorder) — the running order is unchanged")
491		}
492		return w
493	}
494	
495	// reordered reports whether the paths common to BOTH slices appear in a different
496	// relative order in desired than in live. The common subsequence has the same length
497	// on both sides (it is the name intersection), so a positional mismatch is a reorder.
498	func reordered(live, desired []config.Path) bool {
499		desiredNames := make(map[string]struct{}, len(desired))
500		for _, p := range desired {
501			desiredNames[p.Name] = struct{}{}
502		}
503		liveNames := make(map[string]struct{}, len(live))
504		for _, p := range live {
505			liveNames[p.Name] = struct{}{}
506		}
507		var liveCommon, desiredCommon []string
508		for _, p := range live {
509			if _, ok := desiredNames[p.Name]; ok {
510				liveCommon = append(liveCommon, p.Name)
511			}
512		}
513		for _, p := range desired {
514			if _, ok := liveNames[p.Name]; ok {
515				desiredCommon = append(desiredCommon, p.Name)
516			}
517		}
518		for i := range liveCommon {
519			if liveCommon[i] != desiredCommon[i] {
520				return true
521			}
522		}
523		return false
524	}
525	
526	// runningConfig advances the running config to the membership the bind now serves:
527	// the survivors (live paths not removed, keeping their ORIGINAL parameters and order)
528	// followed by the added paths (their new parameters). Non-path fields are carried over
529	// unchanged — a membership-only reload does not re-apply them. It mirrors the m.defs
530	// order the bind rebuilds on a Close→Open (survivors-in-order, then appended adds).
531	func runningConfig(live *config.Config, add []config.Path, remove []string) *config.Config {
532		removeSet := make(map[string]struct{}, len(remove))
533		for _, n := range remove {
534			removeSet[n] = struct{}{}
535		}
536		paths := make([]config.Path, 0, len(live.Paths)+len(add))
537		for _, p := range live.Paths {
538			if _, gone := removeSet[p.Name]; !gone {
539				paths = append(paths, p)
540			}
541		}
542		paths = append(paths, add...)
543		running := *live
544		running.Paths = paths
545		return &running
546	}
547	
548	// diffPaths computes, by name, which desired paths are not yet live (to add) and
549	// which live paths are no longer desired (to remove). It is a pure function so the
550	// reload diff is unit-testable without a running engine.
551	func diffPaths(live []string, desired []config.Path) (add []config.Path, remove []string) {
552		liveSet := make(map[string]struct{}, len(live))
553		for _, n := range live {
554			liveSet[n] = struct{}{}
555		}
556		desiredSet := make(map[string]struct{}, len(desired))
557		for _, p := range desired {
558			desiredSet[p.Name] = struct{}{}
559			if _, ok := liveSet[p.Name]; !ok {
560				add = append(add, p)
561			}
562		}
563		for _, n := range live {
564			if _, ok := desiredSet[n]; !ok {
565				remove = append(remove, n)
566			}
567		}
568		return add, remove
569	}
570	
571	// defaultFailbackDwell is how long a recovered higher-priority path must stay up
572	// before egress fails BACK to it, damping flap-induced thrash (T15 hysteresis).
573	// Unlike the probe-cadence/liveness thresholds (which are the shared
574	// telemetry.Default* single source of truth, D16), the failback dwell is not part
575	// of the failover-recovery budget — failover to a backup is instant — so it stays a
576	// device-local constant.
577	const defaultFailbackDwell = 5 * time.Second
578	
579	// fecConfig maps the validated [fec] config block onto the fec.Config the multipath
580	// Bind consumes (T24), or returns nil when FEC is disabled so the Bind runs the
581	// datapath with no parity plane. The ratio was already range-checked in config
582	// validation; the Bind re-validates defensively.
583	func fecConfig(f config.FEC) *fec.Config {
584		if !f.Enabled {
585			return nil
586		}
587		return &fec.Config{
588			DataShards:   f.DataShards,
589			ParityShards: f.ParityShards,
590			Deadline:     f.Deadline,
591		}
592	}
593	
594	// adaptiveFECConfig maps the [fec] block onto the adaptive controller config the multipath
595	// Bind drives (T29), or returns nil when FEC is disabled or the block did not opt into
596	// adaptive mode — in which case the Bind runs the fixed-ratio plane (T24) unchanged. The
597	// controller uses the simulation-proven default control law (internal/adaptivefec,
598	// DefaultConfig), with only the group geometry pinned to the configured ratio: DataShards
599	// (K) is the FEC data_shards and MaxParity (the parity CEILING) is the FEC parity_shards,
600	// which the receiver's decoder is built at. The Bind re-validates and cross-checks these
601	// against the FEC config defensively.
602	func adaptiveFECConfig(f config.FEC) *adaptivefec.Config {
603		if !f.Enabled || !f.Adaptive {
604			return nil
605		}
606		c := adaptivefec.DefaultConfig()
607		c.DataShards = f.DataShards
608		c.MaxParity = f.ParityShards
609		// Exactly one sizing mode is active (config load enforces mutual exclusion): in the
610		// residual-SLA mode f.SafetyFactor is 0 and f.TargetResidual drives M; in the legacy
611		// mode f.TargetResidual is 0 and f.SafetyFactor (defaulted at load) drives M.
612		c.SafetyFactor = f.SafetyFactor
613		c.TargetResidual = f.TargetResidual
614		return &c
615	}
616	
617	// buildScheduler constructs one live *telemetry.Prober per path and the P1
618	// active-backup send scheduler over them, in cfg.Paths' configured priority order
619	// (index 0 = the preferred primary). The returned probers ARE the scheduler's
620	// PathHealth sources (a *Prober is internally synchronized, satisfying the
621	// PathHealth concurrency contract — a bare *Liveness would not) and are handed to
622	// the bind so the probe transport drives the very same liveness the scheduler
623	// selects on. This replaces the T15 sched.AlwaysUp placeholder with real on-wire
624	// failover (T37).
625	func buildScheduler(cfg *config.Config, lg log.Logger) (sched.Scheduler, []*telemetry.Prober, bind.ProberFactory, error) {
626		clock := telemetry.SystemClock{}
627		proberCfg := telemetry.ProberConfig{
628			LossWindow: telemetry.DefaultLossWindow,
629			Liveness: telemetry.LivenessConfig{
630				DownAfter:        telemetry.DefaultDownAfter,
631				UpAfterSuccesses: telemetry.DefaultUpSuccesses,
632			},
633		}
634		// One random per-boot session id shared by every path's Prober (it identifies
635		// this boot, not the path): a peer restart presents a new session id that resets
636		// the surviving responder's anti-replay high-water so liveness recovers (T38, D12).
637		// A runtime-added path (T30) reuses the SAME session id, so its probes join this
638		// boot's stream and the peer's reflector adopts them without a challenge reset.
639		sessionID, err := telemetry.NewSessionID(rand.Reader)
640		if err != nil {
641			return nil, nil, nil, err
642		}
643		// newProber mints one path's Prober with the shared session/config/clock. It is
644		// the single construction point for boot-time AND runtime paths, so both measure
645		// liveness identically.
646		newProber := func(name string, id uint8) *telemetry.Prober {
647			return telemetry.NewProber(name, id, sessionID, cfg.PSK, proberCfg, clock, lg)
648		}
649		probers := make([]*telemetry.Prober, len(cfg.Paths))
650		health := make([]sched.PathHealth, len(cfg.Paths))
651		quality := make([]sched.PathQuality, len(cfg.Paths))
652		for i := range cfg.Paths {
653			probers[i] = newProber(cfg.Paths[i].Name, uint8(i))
654			health[i] = probers[i]
655			quality[i] = probers[i]
656		}
657		// Policy is a config choice: active-backup (P1, default) or the T21 weighted-
658		// aggregation policy. Both consume the SAME per-path *Prober set — a *Prober
659		// satisfies BOTH PathHealth (liveness) and PathQuality (RTT/loss Estimate) — so the
660		// probe transport drives the very liveness/quality the scheduler selects on, and the
661		// swap is behind config with no Bind change.
662		scheduler, err := selectScheduler(cfg, health, quality, clock, lg)
663		if err != nil {
664			return nil, nil, nil, err
665		}
666		return scheduler, probers, newProber, nil
667	}
668	
669	// selectScheduler builds the send scheduler the configured policy names, over the
670	// per-path health (and, for the weighted policy, quality) sources. active-backup is
671	// the P1 default; weighted is T21. The weighted knobs are validated at config load,
672	// so translating them here cannot fail on range — only NewWeighted's structural
673	// checks (which the wiring satisfies) apply.
674	func selectScheduler(cfg *config.Config, health []sched.PathHealth, quality []sched.PathQuality, clock telemetry.Clock, lg log.Logger) (sched.Scheduler, error) {
675		switch cfg.Scheduler.Policy {
676		case config.PolicyWeighted:
677			sc := cfg.Scheduler
678			return sched.NewWeighted(health, quality, sched.WeightedConfig{
679				PerPathCapacity:   sc.PerPathCapacityFPS,
680				EngageFraction:    sc.EngageFraction,
681				DisengageFraction: sc.DisengageFraction,
682				CollapseDwell:     sc.CollapseDwell,
683				LoadTau:           sc.LoadTau,
684				Pacing:            sc.PacingEnabled,
685				PacingBurst:       sc.PacingBurstFrames,
686				WeightRTTFloor:    sc.WeightRTTFloor,
687				WeightLossFloor:   sc.WeightLossFloor,
688			}, clock, lg)
689		default:
690			// active-backup (and the empty default, normalized to it at config load).
691			return sched.NewActiveBackup(
692				health,
693				sched.Config{FailbackAfter: defaultFailbackDwell},
694				clock,
695				lg,
696			)
697		}
698	}
699	
700	// Name is the created TUN interface name (for external addressing/routing).
701	func (t *Tunnel) Name() string { return t.name }
702	
703	// Wait blocks until the device is torn down (its own Close, or an unrecoverable
704	// engine error).
705	func (t *Tunnel) Wait() { <-t.dev.Wait() }
706	
707	// Close stops the probe loop, brings the device down, and releases the TUN and
708	// Bind. Idempotent. It also releases this tunnel's hold on the single-amnezia-
709	// engine-per-process guard exactly once, so a later Up may reconfigure the
710	// process-global amnezia state. Probing is stopped BEFORE the engine tears the
711	// bind's sockets down so no emission races the close.
712	func (t *Tunnel) Close() {
713		// Shut the scrape endpoint FIRST so no in-flight /metrics scrape reads the Bind while
714		// the engine tears its sockets down. reloadMu serializes this against a concurrent
715		// SIGHUP-driven rebind.
716		t.reloadMu.Lock()
717		t.stopMetricsLocked()
718		t.reloadMu.Unlock()
719		if t.stopProbes != nil {
720			t.stopProbes()
721		}
722		if t.stopReconcile != nil {
723			t.stopReconcile()
724		}
725		if t.stopHubFailover != nil {
726			t.stopHubFailover()
727		}
728		// Stop the DNS re-resolution loop between hub-failover and the engine teardown (T74): no
729		// re-resolve may race the engine peer's install/repoint or the socket close.
730		if t.stopResolution != nil {
731			t.stopResolution()
732		}
733		t.dev.Close()
734		t.releaseOnce.Do(func() { globalAmneziaGuard.release(t.amnezia) })
735	}
736	
737	// engineLogger adapts the amneziawg engine's logger onto wanbond's structured
738	// logger under a "wg" component. The engine is verbose only when the daemon runs
739	// at debug level; otherwise only its errors surface.
740	func engineLogger(lg log.Logger, level string) *awgdevice.Logger {
741		wg := lg.Component("wg")
742		verbosef := func(string, ...any) {}
743		if strings.EqualFold(strings.TrimSpace(level), "debug") {
744			verbosef = func(format string, args ...any) { wg.Debug(fmt.Sprintf(format, args...)) }
745		}
746		return &awgdevice.Logger{
747			Verbosef: verbosef,
748			Errorf:   func(format string, args ...any) { wg.Error(fmt.Sprintf(format, args...)) },
749		}
750	}
751	
752	// bootEndpoint is a peer's initial engine endpoint derived from the bounded boot resolve: the
753	// flattened head of its seeded failoverSpecs, valid only when at least one spec has an expansion.
754	// An invalid boot endpoint means the peer boots endpoint-less (tolerant boot).
755	type bootEndpoint struct {
756		ap    netip.AddrPort
757		valid bool
758	}
759	
760	// bootEndpoints is the outcome of the bounded initial resolve: the resolver constructed for
761	// hostname re-resolution (nil when no peer carries a hostname spec — the wiring is then provably
762	// inert, Q29), the per-peer seeded failoverSpecs (hostname expansions filled from the boot resolve,
763	// literals fixed), and the per-peer boot endpoint the UAPI render installs. specs and endpoints are
764	// indexed 1:1 with cfg.WireGuard.Peers.
765	type bootEndpoints struct {
766		resolver  dnsresolve.Resolver
767		specs     [][]failoverSpec
768		endpoints []bootEndpoint
769	}
770	
771	// resolveBootEndpoints performs the bounded initial hostname resolve (Q30) and derives, per peer,
772	// the seeded failoverSpecs and the boot endpoint the UAPI render installs. It constructs the
773	// resolver AT MOST ONCE, and ONLY when some peer carries a hostname endpoint spec — a config with
774	// zero hostname specs never invokes newResolver, so its wiring is provably inert (Q29). A literal
775	// spec is seeded verbatim (byte-for-byte the pre-G5 behaviour); a hostname spec is resolved under
776	// the [dns] per-lookup timeout and family-filtered. A lookup that fails, times out, or yields no
777	// usable record leaves that spec EMPTY — the peer boots endpoint-less and the re-resolution loop
778	// installs it on first success (R70). A resolver that fails to construct is logged and treated as
779	// absent (every hostname spec then boots empty), since re-resolution must never fail bring-up.
780	func resolveBootEndpoints(cfg *config.Config, newResolver resolverFactory, lg log.Logger) bootEndpoints {
781		peers := cfg.WireGuard.Peers
782		b := bootEndpoints{
783			specs:     make([][]failoverSpec, len(peers)),
784			endpoints: make([]bootEndpoint, len(peers)),
785		}
786	
787		hasName := false
788		for _, p := range peers {
789			for _, s := range p.EndpointSpecs {
790				if s.IsName {
791					hasName = true
792				}
793			}
794		}
795		if hasName {
796			if r, err := newResolver(); err != nil {
797				lg.Warn("dns: could not construct resolver; hostname endpoints boot endpoint-less and will not re-resolve",
798					"error", err.Error())
799			} else {
800				b.resolver = r
801			}
802		}
803	
804		fams := pathFamiliesFromPaths(cfg.Paths)
805		for i, p := range peers {
806			specs := make([]failoverSpec, len(p.EndpointSpecs))
807			for j, s := range p.EndpointSpecs {
808				if !s.IsName {
809					specs[j] = failoverSpec{spec: s, addrs: []netip.AddrPort{s.Addr}}
810					continue
811				}
812				var addrs []netip.AddrPort
813				if b.resolver != nil {
814					addrs = bootResolveHostname(b.resolver, s, fams, cfg.DNS.Timeout, lg)
815				}
816				specs[j] = failoverSpec{spec: s, addrs: addrs}
817			}
818			b.specs[i] = specs
819			// The boot endpoint is the FLATTENED head — the first spec (in TOML order) with a
820			// non-empty expansion. None ⇒ endpoint-less boot.
821			for _, sp := range specs {
822				if len(sp.addrs) > 0 {
823					b.endpoints[i] = bootEndpoint{ap: sp.addrs[0], valid: true}
824					break
825				}
826			}
827		}
828		return b
829	}
830	
831	// bootResolveHostname does one context-bounded lookup for a hostname spec at boot and returns the
832	// ordered, family-filtered []netip.AddrPort (empty on any failure/timeout/empty-result — the caller
833	// then boots that spec endpoint-less). The timeout is the [dns] per-lookup timeout (kept > 0 by
834	// config validation); a non-positive value falls back to an unbounded context, matching
835	// resolution.lookupContext, but Up should never pass one.
836	func bootResolveHostname(resolver dnsresolve.Resolver, s config.EndpointSpec, fams pathFamilies, timeout time.Duration, lg log.Logger) []netip.AddrPort {
837		ctx := context.Background()
838		if timeout > 0 {
839			var cancel context.CancelFunc
840			ctx, cancel = context.WithTimeout(ctx, timeout)
841			defer cancel()
842		}
843		addrs, _, _, err := resolver.Lookup(ctx, s.Host)
844		if err != nil {
845			lg.Info("dns: initial resolve failed; endpoint boots deferred to the re-resolution loop",
846				"host", s.Host, "error", err.Error())
847			return nil
848		}
849		eps := orderAddrPorts(addrs, s.Port, fams)
850		if len(eps) == 0 {
851			lg.Info("dns: initial resolve yielded no usable record for local path families; endpoint boots deferred",
852				"host", s.Host)
853			return nil
854		}
855		return eps
856	}
857	
858	// uapiConfig renders cfg into the newline-delimited UAPI set string the engine's
859	// IpcSet consumes. Keys are lowercase hex (UAPI's on-the-wire encoding), NOT the
860	// base64 form the TOML carries. Amnezia obfuscation keys are emitted only when the
861	// block is configured; an all-zero Amnezia block leaves the engine in plain
862	// WireGuard mode. The same amnezia parameters are applied on BOTH roles (edge and
863	// concentrator) as defense-in-depth — they must match end to end for the handshake
864	// to succeed (config validation makes each end specify a complete profile, D1).
865	func uapiConfig(cfg *config.Config, bootEndpoints []bootEndpoint) (string, error) {
866		var b strings.Builder
867	
868		priv := cfg.WireGuard.PrivateKey.Bytes()
869		fmt.Fprintf(&b, "private_key=%s\n", hex.EncodeToString(priv[:]))
870		if cfg.Role == config.RoleConcentrator {
871			fmt.Fprintf(&b, "listen_port=%d\n", cfg.WireGuard.ListenPort)
872		}
873		writeAmnezia(&b, cfg.Amnezia)
874	
875		for i, peer := range cfg.WireGuard.Peers {
876			pub := peer.PublicKey.Bytes()
877			fmt.Fprintf(&b, "public_key=%s\n", hex.EncodeToString(pub[:]))
878			// The engine peer's endpoint is built ONLY from a resolved flattened entry (Q30): the
879			// boot endpoint is the head of the peer's seeded failoverSpecs — a literal's fixed address
880			// for an all-literal peer (byte-for-byte the pre-G5 render, Endpoints[0]), or a hostname's
881			// boot-resolved head. When nothing resolved (a hostname peer whose name did not resolve in
882			// the boot window, or a resolver-less concentrator peer with no endpoint at all) the boot
883			// endpoint is INVALID and no endpoint line is emitted — the peer boots endpoint-less
884			// (tolerant boot), and for a hostname peer the re-resolution loop installs it on first
885			// success (R70). Switching to a standby endpoint on hub loss is the failover controller's
886			// job, not this initial render.
887			if ep := bootEndpoints[i]; ep.valid {
888				fmt.Fprintf(&b, "endpoint=%s\n", ep.ap.String())
889				// A keepalive keeps the edge->concentrator session warm and lets the
890				// concentrator relearn the edge endpoint after a NAT rebind; only the
891				// initiating (edge) side sets it.
892				fmt.Fprintf(&b, "persistent_keepalive_interval=%d\n", keepaliveSeconds)
893			}
894			if len(peer.AllowedIPs) == 0 {
895				return "", fmt.Errorf("peer %d (%s): at least one allowed_ip is required", i, hex.EncodeToString(pub[:8]))
896			}
897			for _, cidr := range peer.AllowedIPs {
898				fmt.Fprintf(&b, "allowed_ip=%s\n", cidr)
899			}
900		}
901		return b.String(), nil
902	}
903	
904	// keepaliveSeconds is the edge's persistent-keepalive interval.
905	const keepaliveSeconds = 25
906	
907	// writeAmnezia emits the nine amneziawg obfuscation UAPI keys, but only when the
908	// block is configured. When amnezia is unused, wanbond leaves the engine's
909	// PROCESS-GLOBAL message-type state untouched (see the amneziaGuard invariant).
910	// Config validation guarantees a configured block is complete and self-consistent
911	// (D1), and applyDefaults has already filled the standard magic headers (1..4)
912	// when they were omitted, so the emitted set never carries an h*=0 sentinel.
913	func writeAmnezia(b *strings.Builder, a config.Amnezia) {
914		if !a.Configured() {
915			return
916		}
917		fmt.Fprintf(b, "jc=%d\njmin=%d\njmax=%d\ns1=%d\ns2=%d\nh1=%d\nh2=%d\nh3=%d\nh4=%d\n",
918			a.Jc, a.Jmin, a.Jmax, a.S1, a.S2, a.H1, a.H2, a.H3, a.H4)
919	}
920
